Facts.text: Tyrus surrenders to Crusaders
King Charles VII issues the Pragmatic Sanction of Bourges stating that a General Church Council with superior power to the Pope must be held every 10 years
KingFerdinand IIreturns to Naples
French troops invade Luxembourg
Traditional date chocolate thought to have been introduced to Europe
King Henri III and Duke De Guise signs Treaty of Nemours: French Huguenots lose all freedoms
English explorer John Mildenhall leaves Aleppo with a caravan of 600 people bound for Lahore - one of the first Englishmen to reach India overland
People's uprising against high prices and Spanish rule in Naples
Handel's "Te Deum" & "Jubilate" officially premieres in a thanksgiving service celebtrating the Treaty of Utrecht
Battle of Bloody Marsh: Spanish forces assault British colonial forces on Simons Island, Georgia, and are decisively repelled
British parliament grants Jews the right to apply for citizenship
Kings College in NYC opens (renamed Columbia College)
Firm of Johann Buddenbrook founded, in Thomas Mann's novel
American Revolutionary War: Battle of Hubbardton
Quasi-War: the U.S. Congress rescinds treaties with France sparking the 'war'
Ranjit Singh's men take up their positions outside Lahore
First comic book "The Wasp" is published in Hudson, New York, criticizing Republican politicians
Central American federation is dissolved
Scottish explorer Edward Eyre reaches Albany, Western Australia
Battle of Laurel Hill, Virginia
Land Grant Act endows state colleges with federal land
Surrey wicket-keeper Ted Pooley completes a then-1st class cricket record 12 dismissals (8 caught, 4 stumped) in a County match against Sussex at The Oval
27th Postmaster General: James W Marshall of NJ takes office
Hamburg massacre: white farmers attack a black militia in Hamburg, South Carolina with 7 killed
Social-Democratic United forms in Amsterdam
Marcellus F. Berry, an American Express employee is granted four copyrights for what he called "the travelers cheque"
Katipunan: the Revolutionary Philippine Brotherhood is established leading to the fall of the Spanish Empire in Asia
US President McKinley signs the Organic Act to annex Hawaii
Boston Beaneaters pitcher Kid Nichols notches his 300th career MLB victory with an 11-4 win over Chicago Orphans
127°F (53°C), Parker, Arizona (state record until 1994)
A Great Gorge and International Railway trolley with an extreme overload of 157 passengers crashes near Queenston, Ontario, killing 15
The New Zealand Labour party is formed
Philadelphia Phillies tie MLB record of 8 steals in an innings (9th) in a 10-5 loss vs NY Giants
Cleveland Indians set an AL record 27 runs including 13 in the 6th in 27-3 win v Boston Red Sox
University of Delaware invents "junior year abroad" (at Sorbonne)
American Robert LeGendre sets then long jump world record at 25' 5½" in Paris, France
BBC's first disc jockey Christopher Stone begins playing records on the air
American Edward Hamm sets then long jump world record at 25' 11" at Cambridge, Massachusetts
Romania & Vatican sign concord
American businessman Henry F. Phillips receives patents for a new "cross-recessed" screw, and the new screwdriver needed to make it work
RCA broadcasts the first real TV program, a variety show with dancing, a film on locomotives, a Bonwit Teller fashion show, and a monologue from Tobacco Road
Japanese and Chinese troops clash at the Marco Polo Bridge near Beijing, beginning the Second Sino-Japanese War
"The Rules of the Game", French film directed by Jean Renoir, starring Nora Gregor and Paulette Dubost, premieres in Paris
Nazis execute 5,000 Jews in and around Kovono, Lithuania over the course of a week
US forces land in Iceland to forestall Nazi invasion
World War II: Beirut is occupied by Free France and British troops
1st legal New Jersey horse race in 50 years; Garden State Park track opens in Delaware Township (now Cherry Hill)
Germany troop march into Woronezj
3rd day of Battle of Kursk, USSR: Operation Citadel: Germans occupy Dubrova, Luftwaffe fighter ace shoots down 7 Russian aircraft
German Submarine U-951 sunk by depth charges, off Cape St. Vincent in the North Atlantic
Liberator bombers sinks U-517
Heavy Japanese counter offensive on Saipan
RAF Bomber Command drop 2,572 tons of bombs on Caen, France
Mother Frances Xavier Cabrini, Catholic nun (Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart of Jesus,) canonized as 1st American saint
Alleged and disputed Roswell, New Mexico UFO incident
Six female reservists become the first women sworn into the regular US Navy
"Dragnet" premieres on NBC radio; also a TV series in 1951 & 1967
1st Farnborough airshow held
Korean War: UN Security Council establishes the United Nations Command to combat North Korean forces

On her maiden voyage, American ocean liner SS United States crosses Atlantic Ocean in record time of 82 hours 40 minutes, arrives at Bishop Rock off Cornwall, England; she continues to hold the coveted maritime Blue Riband
Formation of the TANU party (Tanganyika African National Union) in Tanzania
Douglas Moore and John Latouche's opera "Ballad of Baby Doe," premieres at the Central City Opera, in Central City, Colorado
Fritz Moravec reaches the peak of Gasherbrum II (8,035 m).
Heavy storm ravages Belgian coast
England cricket batsman Arthur Milton scores an unbeaten 104 in his Test debut, drawn 3rd Test v NZ at Headingley
William Shea outlines plans for a $12M stadium at Flushing Meadows NY
US cemetery officially opens at Margraten, Netherlands
USSR shoots down a US aircraft over Barents sea
James R Hoffa elected chairman of Teamsters
Portuguese cargo ship 'Save" runs aground off of the coast of Quelimane, Mozambique after an onboard fire, exploding two day later
"The Stripper", an instrumental single by trombonist David Rose and his Orchestra goes to No. 1 in the US
Bill Hartack becomes 8th jockey to win 3,000 American thoroughbred races
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
EMI/Parlophone releases The Beatles single "All You Need Is Love" in the UK; it had been introduced to a global audience as part of the BBC's "Our World" television broadcast  12 days earlier
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Canada's House of Commons approves equality of French-English language
German newspaper Der Spiegel reveals Bishop Defregger of Munich is a war criminal
1st women FBI members sworn in (Susan Lynn Roley & Joanne E Pierce)
7 people are killed in separate incidents across Northern Ireland
Dutch Minister of Agt decides to ignore soft drug usage
78 drown as flash flood sweeps a bus into a river (India)
All women board of directors takes control of ABA's Kentucky Colonels
Shoelace Park in the Bronx named
Milwaukee's Don Money sets MLB record for consecutive errorless games at 3rd base (78) in Brewers 8-5 win v Twins
New Zealand imposes a blanket ban on sports teams from South Africa
TV soap opera "Ryan's Hope" premieres
119 women joined the Corps of Cadets, establishing the first class of women in the US Military Academy at West Point
Lt General Vernon A Walters, USA, ends term as deputy director of CIA; succeeded by E Henry Knoche
12,000 police occupy university in Mexico City

NBA approves franchise swap; Buffalo Braves owner John Y. Brown and Harry Mangurian acquire Boston Celtics, while the Celtics owner Irv Levin gets Braves, later moved to San Diego to become the Clippers
Solomon Islands declares independence from the United Kingdom
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Institution of Sharia law in Iran
Jim King completes riding Miracle Strip Roller coaster 368 hours
The Safra massacre in Lebanon
The solar-powered aircraft, Solar Challenger, successfully completes a 163 mile flight across the English Channel
English athlete David Moorcroft beats Kenyan Henry Rono's 5000m world record by nearly 5 seconds, 13:00.41 in Oslo, Norway
11-year-old Samantha Smith of Manchester, Maine, leaves for USSR
5 die in a train crash in Williston, Vermont
IBM-PC DOS Version 3.2 (updated) released
Jordan government shuts al-Fatah offices
Supreme Court strikes down Gramm-Rudman deficit-reduction law
Kiwanis Clubs end men-only tradition, vote to admit women
Five prominent anti-apartheid activists are released in Cape Town, South Africa after being detained for up to two years under the Internal Security Act
Reprise Records releases "Stunt", the fourth studio album by Canadian rock band Barenaked Ladies; their most successful album, it features the #1 hit single "One Week"
Soviet Union launches Phobos 1 to probe Martian moon, the mission is unsuccessful
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Concert by the Grateful Dead with Bruce Hornsby & the Range becomes final event at JFK Stadium in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, six days before structure is condemned
New Jersey Devils sign star Russian defensemen Viacheslav Fetisov & Sergei Starikov to NHL contracts despite criticism they are taking North American jobs
South Africa beats Cameroon 1-0 in Durban in first FIFA sanctioned match after nearly 20 years international isolation, apartheid
Guntis Ulmanis elected president of Latvia
Lenny Dykstra hits bases loaded 2-run double to end Phillies 7, LA Dodgers 6 in 20 innings in Philadelphia
Prodigy announces it will offer Cox newspapers
North Yemenite troops occupy Aden
Memphis Mad Dogs 1st CFL home game (vs BC Lions)
Space shuttle STS-71 (Atlantis 14), lands
Space Shuttle STS 78 (Columbia 20), lands
Tower, Minnesota temperature dips to 24°F
Australian cricketer Karen Rolton smashes an unbeaten 209 to set a new world record for highest score in a women's Test in Australia's 9 wicket win v England at Headingley
A scandal breaks out in the United Kingdom when news reports accuse MI6 of sheltering Abu Qatada, supposed European Al Qaeda leader
The United Communist Party of Armenia is formed
Coordinated terrorist bomb blasts strike London's public transport system during the morning rush hour, killing 52 and injuring 700

Influenced by global Live 8 concerts, G8 leaders pledge to double 2004 levels of aid to Africa from US$25 billion to US$50 billion by the year 2010 and to drop the debt owed by 18 of the most heavily indebted poor countries. Results are mixed.
Cleveland Indians first baseman Travis Hafner becomes first player in MLB history to hit 5 grand slams before the All-Star break when he homers off Baltimore's Kris Benson
Roof of a stand in De Grolsch Veste Stadium in Enschede which was under construction collapses, one killed and 14 injured.
Floods in the Krasnodar region, Russia, kill 140 people
10 people are killed after an air taxi crashes in Soldotna, Alaska
Israel launches a "counter-terrorist operation" dubbed Operation Protective Edge against Hamas in Gaza
Deadlock in battle for Aleppo broken when Russian air strikes cut rebel access to the city, 250,000 people put under siege
In Dallas Texas, lone gunman shots and kills five police officers, wounding others during a protest march against fatal police shootings of African Americans
G-20 summit of world leaders begins in Hamburg, Germany amid violent protests
Tesla Motors produces its first mass-market car, the Model 3
United Nations nuclear weapon ban treaty adopted in New York, without participation of nuclear countries
Greek national elections won by center-right New Democracy party headed by Kyriakos Mitsotakis
Mississippi closes all its beaches due to toxic algae bloom due to flooding of the Mississippi River
Nigeria, Africa' biggest economy, joins the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), in attempt to create world's largest free trade area
India surpasses Russia to become the world's third-worst affected country, daily COVID-19 cases exceeding 20,000 and total cases over 700,000
Texas records more than 10,000 daily cases of COVID-19 for the 1st time
Violent protests in Belgrade, Serbia, at government announcement of weekend lockdown due to COVID-19 surge
Haiti president Jovenel Moïse assassinated in his home in Pétionville, Haiti, state of emergency declared across the country
Stanley Cup Final, Amalie Arena, Tampa, FL: Defending champions Tampa Bay Lightning beat Montreal Canadiens, 1-0 in Game 5; retain title, 4-1
World's biggest sandcastle 21.16 high (69.4 feet), using nearly 5,000 tons of sand completed in Blokhus, Denmark
US scientists begin the search for dark matter with a device in a former gold mine in Lead, South Dakota
US White House announces the last of its chemical weapons has been destroyed in eastern Kentucky, as part of international Chemical Weapons Convention, ending a practice begun in WWI
World's first robot-human press conference takes place in Geneva, Switzerland, with reporters asking "Will you rebel against humans?"
For the first time more than 3 million passengers were recorded passing through US airport security in one day, amid an increase in air travel in the US
French elections produces dramatic unexpected result with left-wing alliance New Popular Front winning most votes but not a majority, far-right National Rally third after winning first round
Heat wave across Western American states breaks temperature records, including in Las Vegas which reaches an all-time record of 120 degrees

Australian jury finds Erin Patterson (50) guilty of murder and attempted murder by mushroom in the poisoning deaths of  three of her in-laws at a luncheon of beef Wellington in her Leongatha, Victoria home in 2023
Drummer Matt Cameron announces his departure from the rock band Pearl Jam after 27 years with the group
Manny Machado of the San Diego Padres has two singles, including his 2,000th MLB career hit in 6-3 loss to the visiting Arizona Diamondbacks at Petco Park
A retrial verdict acquitsJoan of Arcof heresy 25 years after her death
Emperor Maximilian Iorders appointment of a conductor, two bass singers and six choir boys, establishing the Vienna Court Music Ensemble; later includes Vienna Boys Choir
Hernán Cortésand the Tlaxcalans defeat a numerically superior Aztec force in the Battle of Otumba in Mexico
First act of trade between the French and Amerindians whenJacques Cartiertrades items with Micmacs at Chaleur Bay in New Brunswick
Isaac Newtonreceives MA from Trinity College, Cambridge
Bonnie Prince Charlieboards French ship ‘Du Teillay’ at St Nazaire accompanied by the warship Elisabeth bound for Scotland [OS]
Toussaint Louverturedeclares Haitian independence
Walter Scott's novel "Waverley" published
Orders barring Jews from serving under US GeneralUlysses S. Grantare revoked
Jesse Jamesgang robs train in Otterville, Missouri
Elihu Rootjoins the cabinet of PresidentTheodore Roosevelt, serving as United States Secretary of State
The Democratic Party meets in Denver at the start of their convention;William Jennings Bryanis nominated as presidential nominee
American athleteJim Thorpewins 4 of 5 events to win the Pentathlon gold medal at the Stockholm Olympics, medal stripped 1913 (played pro baseball), reinstated 1982
Baltimore Orioles owner Jack Dunn offers future baseball legendBabe Ruth, Ernie Shore & Ben Egan for $10k toConnie Mack(Philadelphia A's); refuses pleading lack of finance

Sliced bread is sold for the first time by the Chillicothe Baking Company in Missouri, using a machine invented byOtto Frederick Rohwedder; described as the greatest forward step in the baking industry since bread was wrapped.
Construction begins on Boulder (Hoover) Dam
Baseball Military All-Star team (includingBob Feller) loses to American League All Stars 5-0
John Maynard Keynestakes his seat in the British House Of Lords as Baron Keynes of Tilton after being knighted
-10] Japanese premierHideki Tojovisits Java
Cleveland Indians stun MLB by signing 42-year-old veteran Negro Leagues pitcherSatchel Paige
Che Guevarasets out on a trip through Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, Panama, Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Honduras, and El Salvador
British runnerJim Peters, 17 minutes ahead of any rival, collapses on the final stadium lap of the Commonwealth Games marathon in Vancouver
"Hancock's Half Hour" premieres as a TV show starringTony Hancockand Sid James, written by Ray Galton and Alan Simpson
20-year old Prince Karim succeeds as Aga Khan IV, the new Nizari imam, after the will of his grandfatherAga Khan IIIbypasses his father Prince Aly Khan
PresidentEisenhowersigned a bill approving Alaskan statehood
Otis Reddingrecords the song "Respect" at Stax Studios in Memphis, Tennessee; it later becomes a signature piece forAretha Franklin
Obscenity ban for "Naked Lunch" byWilliam S. Burroughsoverturned by the Massachusetts Supreme Court after testimony byAllen GinsbergandNorman Mailer
British rock group "Yardbirds" disbands; guitaristJimmy Pagerecruits new members to fill concert commitments - the  new group evolves into Led Zeppelin
The Beatles begin recordingGeorge Harrison's song "Here Comes the Sun" at EMI Studios in London;John Lennonwas absent and did not contribute to the track
MLB CommissionerBowie Kuhnannounces that Negro League players elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame to be given full membership in the Museum
Secret Talks Between IRA and British Government:Gerry Adamsis part of a delegation to London for talks with the British Government
FIFA World Cup Final, Olympiastadion, Munich, Germany: StrikerGerd Müllerscores 43rd minute winner as West Germany beats the Netherlands, 2-1
"The Spy Who Loved Me", 10th James Bond film starringRoger MooreandBarbara Bach, premieres in London
American boxerLarry Holmesscores a 7th round TKO of Scott LeDoux in Bloomington, Minnesota in defense of his WBC heavyweight title
The original line-up of British rock band Led Zeppelin unknowingly performs its final show, at the Eissporthalle in West Berlin, West Germany; drummerJohn Bonhamdies in September
Sandra Day O'Connornominated for the US Supreme Court, 1st female Supreme Court justice by PresidentRonald Reagan
American athleteJackie Joyner-Kerseesets world heptathlon record of 7,148 points in the inaugural Goodwill Games at Moscow
It is reported that British "Culture Club" singerBoy Georgeis being treated for heroin addiction
Soviet General and spy for the USDmitri Polyakovarrested in retirement in Russia (executed 1988)
Lieutenant ColonelOliver Northbegins public testimony at Iran-Contra hearing

Hungary holds a state funeral for composerBéla Bartók, 42 years after his death; his remains are relocated from Hartsdale, New York to Budapest's Farkasréti Cemetery for interment next his wife Ditta, who died in 1982
First 'Three Tenors' concert featuringPlácido Domingo,José CarrerasandLuciano Pavarottiat Baths of Caracalla in Rome; recording becomes world's best-selling classical record
Wimbledon Women's Tennis:Martina Navratilovawins her record ninth Wimbledon singles title, defeating AmericanZina Garrison6-4, 6-1
After undergoing the traditional five-year waiting procedure, Czech tennis starIvan Lendlofficially becomes a US citizen
Nelson Mandelasteps down as President of South Africa
In his first appearance at Daytona since the death of fatherDale EarnhardtSr., Dale Jr. wins Pepsi 400 coming from 7th with 9 laps remaining to beat teammate Michael Waltrip
PopeBenedict XVIissues the Apostolic Letter Summorum Pontificum, removing restrictions on celebrating the old Tridentine Mass

Worldwide charity concert event "Live Earth" takes place at 12 venues on seven continents; performers include:Genesis,Keith Urban;Foo Fighters;John Legend;Melissa Etheridge;Dave MatthewsBand; The Police;Roger Waters;Madonna;Alicia Keys;Garth Brooks; Crowded House; Jack Johnson;Shakira; Soweto Gospel Choir; Angélique Kidjo; UB40; Jack Johnson; Linkin Park;Rihanna; Pharrel Williams;Yusef Islam;Sarah Brightman; and Nunatak

Memorial service held for singerMichael Jacksonat the Staples Center in Los Angeles, California; worldwide audience estimated at over 2.5 billion see performances byMariah Carey,Stevie Wonder,Lionel Richie,Jennifer Hudson, and Shaheen Jafargholi
"Harry Potterand the Deathly Hallows – Part 2", the lastHarry Potterfilm, premieres in London
FIFA Women's World Cup Final, Parc Olympique Lyonnais, Décines-Charpieu: Defending champions US win record 4th title beating the Netherlands, 2-0,Megan RapinoePlayer of the Match
Brazilian PresidentJair Bolsonaroannounces he has tested positive for COVID-19
British Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonannounces his resignation at Downing Street after pressure from, and mass resignations of his ministers
NASA beamsRingo Starr’s voice wishing “peace and love" across the universe via the Deep Space Network in honor of his 83rd birthday
"Superman," directed by James Gunn and starring David Corenswet,Nicholas Hoult, andRachel Brosnahanpremieres at TCL Chinese Theater
French Emperor Napoléon Bonaparte and Russian Tsar Alexander I meet on a raft in the middle of the Neman River and sign the first Treaty of Tilsit
Mary Surratt, Lewis Powell, David Herold and George Atzerodt are executed for their role in the conspiracy to assassinate US PresidentAbraham Lincoln
US Navy's Great White Fleet leaves San Francisco Bay
Shirakawa, 72nd Emperor of Japan (1072-87), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1129)
Sutoku, 75th Emperor of Japan (1123-42), born in Japan (d. 1164)
Anna of Austria, Duchess of Bavaria, daughter of Ferdinand I, born in Prague, Bohemia (d. 1590)
John Sigismund Zápolya, King of Hungary as John II (1540-51, 1556-70) who ruled parts of the Kingdom of Hungary with support from the Ottomans, born in Buda, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1571)
Giovanni Francesco Anerio, Italian composer, choirmaster, and oratorio pioneer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1630)
Thomas Howard, 21st Earl of Arundel, English courtier and art collector (Arundel Marbles), born in Finchingfield, England (d. 1646)
Guru Har Krishan, 8th Guru of Sikhism, born in Kiratpur, India (d. 1664)
Johann Tobias Krebs, German organist and composer, born in Heichelheim, Duchy of Saxe-Weimar (d. 1762)
Arvid Niclas von Höpken, Swedish nobleman, soldier, and composer (Il Re Pastore), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1778)
Ludwig Wenzel Lachnith, Bohemian horn player and composer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia, Habsburg Empire (d. 1820)
Joseph Marie Jacquard, French weaver and merchant who invented the programmable loom (Jacquard loom), forerunner to today's computers, born in Lyon, France (d. 1834)
Antonio Francesco Gaetano S Pacini, Italian-French composer and music publisher, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1866)
William Henry Smith, English bookseller (W H Smith), born in Little Thurlow, England (d. 1865)
Michele Amari, Sicilian patriot, Italian revolutionary and historian, born in Palermo, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1889)
William Scott Ketchum, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Norwalk, Connecticut (d. 1871)
Isaac Fitzgerald Shepard, American General (Union Army), born in Natick, Massachusetts on (d. 1889)
Alfred Pleasonton, American Major General (Union Army), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1897)
James Murrell Shackelford, American lawyer, judge, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Lincoln County, Kentucky (d. 1909)
William M. Browne, American Confederate politician and Brigadier General, born in County Mayo, Ireland (d. 1883)
Charles Tindley, American gospel music composer ("I'll Overcome Someday"), and Methodist minister, born in Berlin, Maryland (d. 1933)
Lillien Jane Martin, American psychologist who founded the world's 1st gerontology clinic in San Francisco, born in Olean, New York (d. 1943)
Albert Vandal, French historian (Le Pacha Bonneval), born in Paris, France (d. 1910)
Ludwig Ganghofer, German writer whose novels were often made into films (Hubertus Castle), born in Kaufbeuren, Bavaria (d. 1920)
José Leite de Vasconcelos, Portuguese archeologist, ethnologist, and writer (Etnografia Portuguesa), born in Ucanha, Portugal (d. 1941)
Nettie Stevens, American geneticist (discovered sex chromosomes), born in Cavendish, Vermont (d. 1912)[1]

Juan Lamote de Grignon, Catalan pianist, composer (La nit de Nadal (Christmas Night); Hespèria), and orchestra leader (Cobla Barcelona; València Municipal Orchestra), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1949)
Erwin Bumke, German jurist, lead German Supreme Court under the Nazis, born in Stolp, Prussia, German Empire (d. 1945)
Jacob Weinberg, Russian-born Jewish composer, born in Odessa, Ukraine (d. 1956)
Zdzislaw Jachimecki, Polish composer and music historian, born in Lwów, Ukraine (d. 1953)
Toivo Kuula, Finnish composer, born in Vehkakoski, Grand Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 1918)
Lion Feuchtwanger, German dramatist and novelist (Der falsche Nero), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1958)
Ernest Farrar, English composer whose career was cut short by WWI, born in London (d. 1918)
Carlo Perrier, Italianmineralogistandchemistwho co-discovered the element technetium, born in Turin, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1948)

Marc Chagall [Moise Shagal], Jewish Belarusan-French modernist painter and stained glass artist (La Mariée; I and the Village), born in Liozna, Russian Empire (now Belarus) (d. 1985) [some sources cite date as  July 6]
Raymond Hatton, American actor (Girls in Prison, Lady Killer), born in Red Oak, Iowa (d. 1971)
Tom Powers, American actor (Station West, Destination Moon), born in Owensboro, Kentucky (d. 1955)
Miroslav Krleža, Croatian poet, novelist and playwright (Ballads of Petrica Kerempuh), born in Zagreb, Croatia-Slavonia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1981)
Anna Luther, American silent screen actress (Sinners in Silk), born in New Jersey (d. 1960)
Earle E. Partridge, United States Air Force 4-star general during Korean War, born in Winchendon, Massachusetts (d. 1990)
Gustav Knuth, German actor (Sissi, Freddy Unter Fremden), born in Brunswick, Germany (d. 1987)
Vittorio De Sica, Italian actor and director (Bicycle Thieves, Sciuscià), born in Sora, Lazio (d. 1974)
James McCartney, British cotton salesman, amateur musician, and father of Beatle Paul McCartney, born in Everton, Liverpool, England (d. 1976)
Simone Beck, French chef, teacher, and cookbook author (with Julia Child - Mastering the Art of French Cooking), born in Normandy, France (d. 1991)
Marcel Rubin, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1995)
Anton Karas, Austrian zither player and composer (The Third Man), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1985)
William Feller, Croatian-American mathematician known for probability theory, born in Zagreb, Austria-Hungary (d. 1970)
Helene Johnson, American Harlem Renaissance poet, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1995)
Robert A. Heinlein, American sci-fi author (4 Hugos, Red Planet), born in Butler, Missouri (d. 1988)
Evert Willem Beth, Dutch mathematician and philosopher (Significs Group), born in Almelo, Netherlands (d. 1964)
Harriette Arnow, American novelist and historian (Dollmaker), born in Monticello, Kentucky (d. 1986)
Revilo P. Oliver, American right-wing advocate who alleged Lee Harvey Oswald was part of a Soviet conspiracy, born near r Corpus Christi, Texas (d. 1994)
Billy Herman, American Baseball HOF second baseman (10 x MLB All Star; Brooklyn Dodgers, Chicago Cubs), born in New Albany, Indiana (d. 1992)
Eddie Mayehoff, American actor (Hour Glass, That's My Boy, How to Murder Your Wife), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1992)
Gottfried von Cramm, German tennis player (French C'ship 1934, 36), born in Nettlingen, Germany (d. 1976)
Gian-Carlo Menotti, Italian pianist, librettist, and composer (Amahl & Night Visitors), and two-time Pulitzer Prize winner, born in Cadegliano, Italy (d. 2007)
Joan Perry [Elizabeth Rosiland Miller], American film actress, model, and singer (Hands of a Stranger), born in Pensacola, Florida (d. 1996)
(Joe) "Pinetop" Perkins, American blues pianist and Member of the Blues Hall of Fame, born in Belzoni, Mississippi (d. 2011)
Cor de Groot, Dutch pianist, conductor and composer (Old-Dutch Suite), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Serafim Tulikov, Russian composer (March of the Soviet Youth), born in Kaluga, Russia (d. 2004)
Margaret Walker, American poet and novelist of the Chicago Black Renaissance (For My People), born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 1998)
Lloyd "Tiny" Grimes, American jazz and R&B guitarist (Art Tatum), born in Newport News, Virginia (d. 1989)
Luc Peire, Belgian painter and graphic artist, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1994)
Robert Stevens Baker, American organist, composer, and educator (Union Theological Seminary, 1961-72; Institute of Sacred Music at Yale, 1973-75), born in Pontiac, Illinois (d. 2005)
(Woodrow Wilson) "Red" Sovine, American country music singer ("Phantom 309"; "Teddy Bear"), born in Charleston, West Virginia (d. 1980)
Elton Britt [Baker], American country singer-songwriter ("There's a Star-Spangled Banner Waving Somewhere"; Saturday Night Jamboree), born in Zack, Arkansas (d. 1972)
Fidel Sánchez Hernández, Salvadoran general and politician, President of El Salvador (1967-72), born in El Divisadero, El Salvador (d. 2003)
Lawrence F. O'Brien, American political strategist (Watergate conspirators broke into his office, in-charge of JFK's presidential campaign), born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1990)[1]
Jon Pertwee, English actor and entertainer (Third Doctor in "Doctor Who"; 128 episodes, Worzel Gummidge), born in London (d. 1996)
William Kunstler, American defense attorney (defended the Chicago 7), born in New York City (d. 1995)
Adolf von Thadden, German politician and possible MI6 agent, born in Gut Trieglaf, Germany (d. 1996)
Stanislaw Wislocki, Polish conductor, born in Rzeszów, Poland (d. 1998)
Artie Malvin, American vocalist (Glenn Miller; Jimmy Dorsey), arranger and music director (Julie LaRosa; Pat Boone; Carol Burnett), born in New York City (d. 2006)
Eduardo Falú, Argentine folk music guitarist and composer, born in El Galpón, Argentina (d. 2013)
Roberto Caamaño, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1993)
Dieter Nowka, German composer, conductor and musicologist, born in Madlow, Germany (d. 1998)
Mary Ford [Iris Colleen Summers], American vocalist (with Les Paul - "How High The Moon"), born in El Monte, California (d. 1977)
Wally Phillips, American radio personality known for Chicago's WGN's morning radio show, born in Portsmouth, Ohio (d. 2008)
Yvonne Mitchell, English actress and writer (Johnny Nobody, Genghis Khan), born in London, England (d. 1979)
Nuon Chea [Lau Kim Korn], Cambodian revolutionary and Khmer Rouge leader 'Brother Number Two', born in Battambang, Cambodia (d. 2019)
(Carl) "Doc" Severinsen, American trumpeter and bandleader (The Tonight Show, 1962-92), born in Arlington, Oregon
Alan J. Dixon, American politician (Sen-D-Illinois, 1981-93), born in Belleville, Illinois (d. 2014)
Charlie Louvin [Loudermilk], American country singer-songwriter, and guitarist (Louvin Brothers - "The Only Way Out (Is to Walk Over Me)"), born in Section, Alabama (d. 2011)
Ali Sadikin, Indonesian politician and governor of Jakarta (1966-77), born in Sumedang, West Java, Dutch East Indies (d. 2008)
Patricia Hitchcock, British-American actress, born in London, England (d. 2021)
Hasan Abidi, Pakistani journalist and Urdu poet, born in Zafarabad, British India (d. 2005)
Marcel Liebman, Belgian Marxist historian, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1986)
